q: The half-life of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi>Au</mi><mprescripts></mprescripts><none></none><mn>198</mn></mmultiscripts></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> days. If atomic weight of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi>Au</mi><mprescripts></mprescripts><none></none><mn>198</mn></mmultiscripts></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>198</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, then the activity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mg</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi>Au</mi><mprescripts></mprescripts><none></none><mn>198</mn></mmultiscripts></math> is [in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>disintegration</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>second</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>]:
question: The half-life of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi>Au</mi><mprescripts></mprescripts><none></none><mn>198</mn></mmultiscripts></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> days. If atomic weight of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi>Au</mi><mprescripts></mprescripts><none></none><mn>198</mn></mmultiscripts></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>198</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, then the activity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mg</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi>Au</mi><mprescripts></mprescripts><none></none><mn>198</mn></mmultiscripts></math> is [in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>disintegration</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>second</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>]:
solution:
A=λN

λ=ln2t1/2=ln23×24×60×60 sec-1=2.67×10-6 sec-1

N= Number of atoms in 2 mg Au

=2×10-3198×6×1023=6.06×1015

A=λN=1.618×1013=16.18×1012 dps

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I: For all elements, greater the mass of the nucleus, greater is the binding energy per nucleon.
Statement II: For all elements, nuclei with less binding energy per nucleon transforms to nuclei with greater binding energy per nucleon.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I: For all elements, greater the mass of the nucleus, greater is the binding energy per nucleon.
Statement II: For all elements, nuclei with less binding energy per nucleon transforms to nuclei with greater binding energy per nucleon.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
options: Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
solution:
Statement I is false because binding energy per nucleon does not increase monotonically with mass number. It peaks at Iron-56 and decreases for heavier elements.
Statement II is true. It describes the fundamental principle of nuclear reactions: fission of heavy nuclei (low BE/A) and fusion of light nuclei (low BE/A) both produce nuclei with higher binding energy per nucleon, releasing energy in the process. Both processes are energetically favorable for the same reason: moving toward higher stability (higher BE/A).

subject: Physics
q: The counting rate observed from a radioactive source at $t=0$ was 1600 counts $\mathrm{s}^{-1}$, and $t=8 \mathrm{~s}$, it was 100 counts $\mathrm{s}^{-1}$. The counting rate observed as counts $\mathrm{s}^{-1}$ at $t=6 \mathrm{~s}$ will be
question: The counting rate observed from a radioactive source at $t=0$ was 1600 counts $\mathrm{s}^{-1}$, and $t=8 \mathrm{~s}$, it was 100 counts $\mathrm{s}^{-1}$. The counting rate observed as counts $\mathrm{s}^{-1}$ at $t=6 \mathrm{~s}$ will be
solution:
As we know,
$$
\begin{aligned}
& {\left[\frac{N}{N_0}\right]=\left[\frac{1}{2}\right]^n} \\
& n=\text { no. of half life } \\
& N \text { - no. of atoms left } \\
& N_0 \text { - initial no. of atoms } \\
& \text { By radioactive decay law, } \\
& \frac{d N}{d \mathrm{t}}=k N \\
& k-\operatorname{disin} t e g r a t i o n \text { constant } \\
& \therefore \frac{\frac{d N}{d t}}{\frac{d N_0}{d t}}=\frac{N}{N_0}
\end{aligned}
$$
From (i) and (ii) we get
$$
\frac{\frac{d N}{d t}}{\frac{d N_0}{d t}}=\left[\frac{1}{2}\right]^n
$$
or, $\left[\frac{100}{1600}\right]=\left[\frac{1}{2}\right]^n \Rightarrow\left[\frac{1}{2}\right]^4=\left[\frac{1}{2}\right]^n$
$\therefore n=4$, Therefore, in 8 seconds 4 half life had occurred in which counting rate reduces to 100 counts $\mathrm{s}^{-1}$.
$\therefore$ Half life, $\frac{T_1}{2}=2 \mathrm{sec}$
In $6 \mathrm{sec}, 3$ half life will occur
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \therefore\left[\frac{\frac{d \mathrm{~N}}{d t}}{1600}\right]=\left[\frac{1}{2}\right]^3 \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{d \mathrm{~N}}{d t}=200 \text { counts } \mathrm{s}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Physics
q:
Energy released when two deuterons $\left({ }_1 \mathrm{H}^2\right)$ fuse to form a helium nucleus $\left({ }_2 \mathrm{He}^4\right)$ is :
(Given : Binding energy per nucleon of ${ }_1 \mathrm{H}^2=1.1 \mathrm{MeV}$ and binding energy per nucleon of ${ }_2 \mathrm{He}^4=7.0 \mathrm{MeV}$)
question:
Energy released when two deuterons $\left({ }_1 \mathrm{H}^2\right)$ fuse to form a helium nucleus $\left({ }_2 \mathrm{He}^4\right)$ is :
(Given : Binding energy per nucleon of ${ }_1 \mathrm{H}^2=1.1 \mathrm{MeV}$ and binding energy per nucleon of ${ }_2 \mathrm{He}^4=7.0 \mathrm{MeV}$)
solution: $\begin{aligned} & E_B=\mathrm{BE}_{\text {reactant }}-\mathrm{BE}_{\text {product }} \\ & =1.1 \times 2+1.1 \times 2-7 \times 4=-23.6 \mathrm{MeV} \\ & =\mathrm{Q}=23.6 \mathrm{MeV}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: The energy released when $\dfrac{7}{17.13}$ kg of $^{7}_{3}\text{Li}$ is converted into $^{4}_{2}\text{He}$ by proton bombardment is $\alpha \times 10^{32}$ eV. The value of $\alpha$ is _______. (Nearest integer) (Mass of $^{7}_{3}\text{Li} = 7.0183$ u, mass of $^{4}_{2}\text{He} = 4.004$ u, mass of proton $= 1.008$ u and $1$ u $= 931$ MeV/c$^2$ and Avogadro number $= 6.0 \times 10^{23}$)
question: The energy released when $\dfrac{7}{17.13}$ kg of $^{7}_{3}\text{Li}$ is converted into $^{4}_{2}\text{He}$ by proton bombardment is $\alpha \times 10^{32}$ eV. The value of $\alpha$ is _______. (Nearest integer) (Mass of $^{7}_{3}\text{Li} = 7.0183$ u, mass of $^{4}_{2}\text{He} = 4.004$ u, mass of proton $= 1.008$ u and $1$ u $= 931$ MeV/c$^2$ and Avogadro number $= 6.0 \times 10^{23}$)
solution:
The nuclear reaction for the proton bombardment of Lithium is:

$^{7}_{3}\text{Li} + ^{1}_{1}\text{H} \rightarrow 2 ^{4}_{2}\text{He}$

The mass defect ($\Delta m$) for the reaction is:

$\Delta m = m(^{7}_{3}\text{Li}) + m(^{1}_{1}\text{H}) - 2m(^{4}_{2}\text{He})$

$\Delta m = 7.0183 + 1.008 - 2(4.004)$

$\Delta m = 8.0263 - 8.008 = 0.0183 \text{ u}$

Energy released per reaction ($Q$) is:

$Q = \Delta m \times 931 \text{ MeV} = 0.0183 \times 931 \text{ MeV} = 17.0373 \text{ MeV}$

Number of moles of $^{7}_{3}\text{Li}$ in $\dfrac{7}{17.13} \text{ kg}$:

$n = \dfrac{\dfrac{7}{17.13} \times 10^3 \text{ g}}{7 \text{ g/mol}} = \dfrac{1000}{17.13} \text{ mol}$

Number of atoms of $^{7}_{3}\text{Li}$:

$N = n \times N_A = \dfrac{1000}{17.13} \times 6.0 \times 10^{23} = \dfrac{6}{17.13} \times 10^{26}$

Total energy released ($E$) is:

$E = N \times Q = \left(\dfrac{6}{17.13} \times 10^{26}\right) \times (17.0373 \times 10^6 \text{ eV})$

$E = \dfrac{102.2238}{17.13} \times 10^{32} \text{ eV} \approx 5.967 \times 10^{32} \text{ eV}$

Rounding to the nearest integer, we get $\alpha = 6$.

Answer: $6$

A=A0e-λt

A08=A0e-λt⇒λt=ln8

λt=3ln2

ln2λ=t3=303=10 years

q: If a radioactive element having half-life of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn></math> min is undergoing beta decay, the fraction of radioactive element remains undecayed after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>90</mn></math> min will be :
question: If a radioactive element having half-life of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn></math> min is undergoing beta decay, the fraction of radioactive element remains undecayed after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>90</mn></math> min will be :
solution:
Given: Half life of radioactive element t12=30 min.

In 90 min, there will be three half lives, so n=3.

Using radioactive decay law, fraction of radioactive element remains undecayed is 

NN0=12n=123

⇒NN0=18

subject: Physics
q:
Consider the following radioactive decay process:

A84218⟶αA1⟶β-A2⟶γA3⟶αA4⟶β+A5⟶γA6

The mass number and the atomic number of A6 are given by:
question:
Consider the following radioactive decay process:

A84218⟶αA1⟶β-A2⟶γA3⟶αA4⟶β+A5⟶γA6

The mass number and the atomic number of A6 are given by:
solution:
When an alpha particle is emitted from nucleus the nucleus loses two proton and two neutrons. This means the atomic mass number decreases by 4 and the atomic number decreases by 2.

In beta decays, the mass number of the atoms remains the same, but the atomic number changes: the atomic number increases in the negative beta decay and decreases in the positive beta decay by 1, respectively.

In gamma decay, mass number and atomic number remains unchanged.

Step by step variation is given below:

 84218A⟶α 82214A1⟶β- 83214A2⟶γ 83214A3⟶α 81210A4⟶β+ 80210A5⟶γ 80210A6

subject: Physics
q: A free neutron decays into a proton but a free proton does not decay into neutron. This is because
question: A free neutron decays into a proton but a free proton does not decay into neutron. This is because
options: neutron is a composite particle made of a proton and an electron
neutron has larger rest mass than proton
solution:
Mass of the neutron is mn=1.67493×10-27 kg,

mass of the proton is mp=1.67262×10-27 kg.

Proton's rest mass is less than that of a neutron's rest mass. As particle can not decay to a state of greater mass, hence the proton does not decay to neutron outside nucleus whereas the neutron can decay into a proton though β-decay.

subject: Physics
q: An atom ${ }_{3}^{8} X$ is bombarded by shower of fundamental particles and in 10 s this atom absorbed 10 electrons, 10 protons and 9 neutrons. The percentage growth in the surface area of the nucleons is recorded by:
question: An atom ${ }_{3}^{8} X$ is bombarded by shower of fundamental particles and in 10 s this atom absorbed 10 electrons, 10 protons and 9 neutrons. The percentage growth in the surface area of the nucleons is recorded by:
solution:
Surface area $x \propto A^{2/3}$
$X_i = 8^{2/3} K = 4K$
$X_f = (8 + 10 + 9)^{2/3} K = 9K$
% increase in surface area of nucleus
$x_i = \dfrac{9K - 4K}{4K} \times 100 = 125\%$

subject: Physics
q:
Consider the following statements:
A. Atoms of each element emit characteristics spectrum.
B. According to Bohr's Postulate, an electron in a hydrogen atom revolves in a certain stationary orbit.

C. The density of nuclear matter depends on the size of the nucleus.
D. A free neutron is stable but a free proton decay is possible.
E. Radioactivity is an indication of the instability of nuclei. Choose the correct answer from the options given below.
question:
Consider the following statements:
A. Atoms of each element emit characteristics spectrum.
B. According to Bohr's Postulate, an electron in a hydrogen atom revolves in a certain stationary orbit.

C. The density of nuclear matter depends on the size of the nucleus.
D. A free neutron is stable but a free proton decay is possible.
E. Radioactivity is an indication of the instability of nuclei. Choose the correct answer from the options given below.
solution:
(A) True, an atom of each element emits a characteristic spectrum.

(B) True, according to Bohr's postulates mvr=nh2π and hence electron resides into orbits of specific radius called stationary orbits.

(C) False, the density of the nucleus is constant.

(D) False, A free neutron is unstable decays into proton and electron and antineutrino.

(E) True unstable nucleus show radioactivity.

subject: Physics
q:
A nucleus of mass $M+\Delta m$ is at rest and decays into two daughter nuclei of equal mass $\frac{M}{2}$ each. Speed of light is $\mathrm{C}$.
The speed of daughter nuclei is
question:
A nucleus of mass $M+\Delta m$ is at rest and decays into two daughter nuclei of equal mass $\frac{M}{2}$ each. Speed of light is $\mathrm{C}$.
The speed of daughter nuclei is
solution:
Conserving the momentum
$$
\begin{gathered}
0=\frac{\mathrm{M}}{2} \mathrm{~V}_1-\frac{\mathrm{M}}{2} \mathrm{~V}_2 \\
\mathrm{~V}_1=\mathrm{V}_2 \quad \quad \ldots \ldots (1) \\
\Delta \mathrm{mc}^2=\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{M}}{2} \mathrm{~V}_1^2+\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{M}}{2} \cdot \mathrm{V}_2^2 \quad \quad \ldots \ldots (2)\\
\Delta \mathrm{mc}^2=\frac{\mathrm{M}}{2} \mathrm{~V}_1^2 \\
\frac{2 \Delta \mathrm{mc}^2}{\mathrm{M}}=\mathrm{V}_1^2 \\
\mathrm{~V}_1=\mathrm{c} \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta \mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{M}}}
\end{gathered}
$$

The half life of the radioactive substance expressed in days is ________.

The half life of the radioactive substance expressed in days is ________.
solution:
N0⟶t12N02⟶t12N04⟶t12N08⟶t12N016

4×t12=80

t12=20 days

subject: Physics
q: For a nucleus of mass number $A$ and radius $R$, the mass density of nucleus can be represented as
question: For a nucleus of mass number $A$ and radius $R$, the mass density of nucleus can be represented as
solution:
The mass density ( $\rho$) of a nucleus is defined as the ratio of its mass to its volume:
$\rho=$ Mass of nucleus / Volume of nucleus
For a spherical nucleus with radius R , the volume is:
$\text { Volume }=(4 / 3) \pi R^3$
The mass of the nucleus is related to the mass number A :
$\text { Mass }-\mathrm{A} \times \mathrm{m} \text { _u }$
(where m_u is the atomic mass unit, approximately $1.66 \times 10^{-27} \mathrm{~kg}$)
Therefore:
$\rho=\left(\mathrm{A} \times \mathrm{m} \_\mathrm{u}\right) /\left[(4 / 3) \pi \mathrm{R}^3\right]$
Now, there's an important relationship between the mass number A and nuclear radius R. Empirically, it has been found that:
$\mathrm{R}=\mathrm{Ro} * \mathrm{~A}^{\wedge}(1 / 3)$
Where Ro is a constant approximately equal to $1.2 \times 10^{-15} \mathrm{~m}(1.2$ fermi).
Substituting this into the density equation:
$\begin{aligned}
& \rho=\left(\mathrm{A} \times \mathrm{m} \_\mathrm{u}\right) /\left[(4 / 3) \pi\left(\mathrm{Ro} \times \mathrm{A}^{\wedge}(1 / 3)\right)^3\right] \\ & \rho=(\mathrm{A} \times \mathrm{m} \text { _u }) /\left[(4 / 3) \pi \mathrm{Ro}^3 \times \mathrm{A}\right] \\ & \rho=\mathrm{m} \_\mathrm{u} /\left[(4 / 3) \pi \mathrm{Ro}^3\right]
\end{aligned}$
This shows that the nuclear density is approximately constant for all nuclei, regardless of their mass number. This is a fundamental property of nuclear matter known as nuclear saturation density.
The numerical value is:
$\rho=2.3 \times 10^{17} \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$

q: Two radioactive materials <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> have decay constants <math><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">λ</mi></math> and <math><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">λ</mi></math> , respectively. If initially they have the same number of nuclei, then the ratio of the number of nuclei of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> to that of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> will be <math><mn>1</mn><mo>/</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></math> after a time:
question: Two radioactive materials <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> have decay constants <math><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">λ</mi></math> and <math><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">λ</mi></math> , respectively. If initially they have the same number of nuclei, then the ratio of the number of nuclei of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> to that of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> will be <math><mn>1</mn><mo>/</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></math> after a time:
solution:
NA=N0e-10λt
NB=N0e-λt
NANB=1e=e-10λt+λt=e-9λt
1=9λt
t=19λ

subject: Physics
q:
For a nucleus XZA having mass number A and atomic number Z

A. The surface energy per nucleon (bs)=–a1A23.

B. The Coulomb contribution to the binding energy bc=−a2Z(Z−1)A43.

C. The volume energy bv = a3A

D. Decrease in the binding energy is proportional to surface area.

E. While estimating the surface energy, it is assumed that each nucleon interacts with 12 nucleons. (a1, a2 and a3 are constants)

Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
question:
For a nucleus XZA having mass number A and atomic number Z

A. The surface energy per nucleon (bs)=–a1A23.

B. The Coulomb contribution to the binding energy bc=−a2Z(Z−1)A43.

C. The volume energy bv = a3A

D. Decrease in the binding energy is proportional to surface area.

E. While estimating the surface energy, it is assumed that each nucleon interacts with 12 nucleons. (a1, a2 and a3 are constants)

Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
solution:
The formula to calculate the radius of a nucleus is given by

R=R0A13   ...1

Hence, the volume energy can be written as

bv=C1R3= a3A133=a3A

The nuclear binding energy is the minimum energy that is required to disassemble the nucleus of an atom into its constituent protons and neutrons. Also, the energy of the nucleus is negative with regard to the energy of the particles pulled apart to infinite distance, because energy must be utilised to split a nucleus into its individual protons and neutrons. It is proportional to the surface area.

Hence, statements C and D are most appropriate.

q: How many alpha and beta particles are emitted when Uranium <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">U</mi><mprescripts/><mn>92</mn><none/></mmultiscripts><mn>238</mn></msup></math> decays to lead <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mmultiscripts><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>Pb</mi></mrow><mprescripts/><mn>82</mn><none/></mmultiscripts><mn>206</mn></msup></math>?
question: How many alpha and beta particles are emitted when Uranium <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">U</mi><mprescripts/><mn>92</mn><none/></mmultiscripts><mn>238</mn></msup></math> decays to lead <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mmultiscripts><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>Pb</mi></mrow><mprescripts/><mn>82</mn><none/></mmultiscripts><mn>206</mn></msup></math>?
solution:
U92238→Pb82206

Change in mass number is 4α=238-206=32⇒α=8

Change in atomic number is -2α+β=-10⇒-16+β=-10⇒β=6

subject: Physics
q:
In the following nuclear reaction,
D→αD1→β-D2→αD3→γD4
Mass number of D is 182 and atomic number is 74 . Mass number and atomic number of D4 respectively will be
question:
In the following nuclear reaction,
D→αD1→β-D2→αD3→γD4
Mass number of D is 182 and atomic number is 74 . Mass number and atomic number of D4 respectively will be
solution:
Equivalent reaction can be written as D→D4+2α+β-+γ

So, mass number of D4=mass number of D-2×4=182-8=174

Atomic number of D4=atomic number of D-2×2+1=74-4+1=71

The reaction is 

D74182⟶αD172178⟶β-D273178⟶αD371174→γD471174

subject: Physics
q: A radio active material is reduced to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>8</mn></mfrac></math> of its original amount in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> days. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> of the material is left after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> days the initial amount of the material is
question: A radio active material is reduced to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>8</mn></mfrac></math> of its original amount in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> days. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> of the material is left after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> days the initial amount of the material is
solution:
The initial and final amount of the radioactive substance are related by the formula

NN0=12n   ...1

where, n is the number of half lives.

Substitute 18 for NN0 into equation (1) and solve to calculate the value of n.

18=12n⇒123=12n⇒n=3

The half-life of the radioactive element can be calculated as follows:

tt12=n   ...2

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (2) and solve to calculate the half-life.

3 dayst12= 3⇒t12= 1 day

The initial and the final mass of the radioactive element are related by

mm0=12t't12   ...3

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (3) and solve to calculate the initial mass of the substance.

8×10-3 kgm0= 1251⇒m0= 25×8×10-3 kg= 256 g

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following nuclear fragments corresponding to nuclear fission between neutron $\left({ }_0^1 \mathrm{n}\right)$ and uranium isotope $\left({ }_{92}^{235} \mathrm{U}\right)$ is correct :
question: Which of the following nuclear fragments corresponding to nuclear fission between neutron $\left({ }_0^1 \mathrm{n}\right)$ and uranium isotope $\left({ }_{92}^{235} \mathrm{U}\right)$ is correct :
solution:
Balancing mass number and atomic number
${ }_{92}^{235} \mathrm{U}+{ }_0^1 \mathrm{n} \rightarrow{ }_{56}^{144} \mathrm{Ba}+{ }_{36}^{89} \mathrm{Kr}+3{ }_0^1 \mathrm{n}$

subject: Physics
q: A nucleus has mass number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and volume <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>. Another nucleus has mass number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and volume <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. If relation between mass number is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>V</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>V</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></mfrac><mo>=</mo></math> _______.
question: A nucleus has mass number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and volume <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>. Another nucleus has mass number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and volume <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. If relation between mass number is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>V</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>V</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></mfrac><mo>=</mo></math> _______.
solution:
For a nucleus we can write volume: V=43πR3

Now, R=R0A13

Therefore, V=43πR03A

⇒V2V1=A2A1=4

subject: Physics
q:
A nucleus with $\mathrm{Z}=92$ emits the following in a sequence:
$\alpha, \beta^{-}, \beta^{-} \alpha, \alpha, \alpha, \alpha, \alpha, \beta^{-}, \beta^{-}, \alpha, \beta^{+}, \beta^{+}, \alpha$. Then $\mathrm{Z}$ of the resulting nucleus is
question:
A nucleus with $\mathrm{Z}=92$ emits the following in a sequence:
$\alpha, \beta^{-}, \beta^{-} \alpha, \alpha, \alpha, \alpha, \alpha, \beta^{-}, \beta^{-}, \alpha, \beta^{+}, \beta^{+}, \alpha$. Then $\mathrm{Z}$ of the resulting nucleus is
solution:
No. of $\alpha$ particles emitted $=8, \quad$ No. of $\beta^{-}$particles emitted $=4, \quad$ No.of $\beta^{+}$particles emitted $=2$
$z=92-2 \times 8+4-2=78$

subject: Physics
q: A radioactive sample has an average life of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi>ms</mi></math> and is decaying. A capacitor of capacitance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math> is first charged and later connected with resistor <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the ratio of the charge on the capacitor to the activity of the radioactive sample is fixed with respect to time then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> should be<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>_______Ω</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
question: A radioactive sample has an average life of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi>ms</mi></math> and is decaying. A capacitor of capacitance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math> is first charged and later connected with resistor <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the ratio of the charge on the capacitor to the activity of the radioactive sample is fixed with respect to time then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> should be<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>_______Ω</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
Tm=30 ms
C=200 μF
qN=Q0e-tRCN0e-λ,t=Q0 N0eλ-1RC

Since qN is constant hence
λ=1RC
R=1λC=TmC=30×10-3200×10-6=150 Ω

subject: Physics
q: A nuclear transformation is denoted by $X(n, \alpha){ }_3^7 \mathrm{Li}$. Which of the following is the nucleus of element $X$ ?
question: A nuclear transformation is denoted by $X(n, \alpha){ }_3^7 \mathrm{Li}$. Which of the following is the nucleus of element $X$ ?
solution: $\mathrm{X}+{ }_0^1 \mathrm{n} \rightarrow{ }_2^4 \mathrm{He}+{ }_3^7 \mathrm{Li}$

subject: Physics
q: The half-life period of a radio-active element $\mathrm{X}$ is same as the mean life time of another radioactive element $\mathrm{Y}$. Initially they have the same number of atoms. Then
question: The half-life period of a radio-active element $\mathrm{X}$ is same as the mean life time of another radioactive element $\mathrm{Y}$. Initially they have the same number of atoms. Then
options: $\mathrm{X}$ and $\mathrm{Y}$ have same decay rate initially
$\mathrm{X}$ and $\mathrm{Y}$ decay at same rate always.
solution:
$\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}=\frac{\ln 2}{\lambda_{\mathrm{x}}}$
$\tau_{\text {mean }}=\frac{1}{\lambda_{\mathrm{y}}} ; \frac{\mathrm{dN}}{\mathrm{dt}}=-\lambda \mathrm{N}$
$\frac{\ln 2}{\lambda_{\mathrm{x}}}=\frac{1}{\lambda_{\mathrm{y}}} \Rightarrow \lambda_{\mathrm{x}}=\lambda_{\mathrm{y}}(0.6932) \Rightarrow \lambda_{\mathrm{y}}>\lambda_{\mathrm{x}}$

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following pair of nuclei are isobars of the element?
question: Which of the following pair of nuclei are isobars of the element?
solution:
Isobars are atoms of different chemical elements that have the same number of nucleons (mass number $A$), but different atomic numbers ($Z$).
In the given options:
(1) $_1^3\text{H}$ and $_2^3\text{He}$: Both have the same mass number $A = 3$. Since they have different atomic numbers ($Z=1$ and $Z=2$), they are isobars.
(2) $_1^2\text{H}$ and $_1^3\text{H}$: These have the same atomic number ($Z=1$) but different mass numbers. They are isotopes.
(3) $_{80}^{198}\text{Hg}$ and $_{79}^{197}\text{Au}$: These have different mass numbers ($198$ and $197$).
(4) $_{92}^{236}\text{U}$ and $_{92}^{238}\text{U}$: These have the same atomic number ($Z=92$) but different mass numbers. They are isotopes.
Thus, the pair $_1^3\text{H}$ and $_2^3\text{He}$ are isobars.

$T_{a v}=\frac{T_\alpha T_\beta}{T_\alpha+T_\beta}$
If $\alpha$ and $B$ are emitted simultaneously.

subject: Physics
q:
The disintegration energy $Q$ for the nuclear fission of ${ }^{235} \mathrm{U} \rightarrow{ }^{140} \mathrm{Ce}+{ }^{94} \mathrm{Zr}+n$ is ______ $\mathrm{MeV}$.
Given atomic masses of ${ }^{235} \mathrm{U}: 235.0439 u ;{ }^{140} \mathrm{Ce}: 139.9054 u$,
${ }^{94} \mathrm{Zr}: 93.9063 u ; n: 1.0086 u \text {, }$
Value of $c^2=931 \mathrm{MeV} / \mathrm{u}$
question:
The disintegration energy $Q$ for the nuclear fission of ${ }^{235} \mathrm{U} \rightarrow{ }^{140} \mathrm{Ce}+{ }^{94} \mathrm{Zr}+n$ is ______ $\mathrm{MeV}$.
Given atomic masses of ${ }^{235} \mathrm{U}: 235.0439 u ;{ }^{140} \mathrm{Ce}: 139.9054 u$,
${ }^{94} \mathrm{Zr}: 93.9063 u ; n: 1.0086 u \text {, }$
Value of $c^2=931 \mathrm{MeV} / \mathrm{u}$
solution:
${ }^{235} \mathrm{U} \rightarrow{ }^{140} \mathrm{Ce}+{ }^{94} \mathrm{Zr}+\mathrm{n}$
Disintegration energy $\begin{aligned} \mathrm{Q} & =\left(\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{R}}-\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{p}}\right) \mathrm{c}^2 \\ \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{R}} & =235.0439 \mathrm{u} \\ \mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{p}} & =139.9054 \mathrm{u}+93.9063 \mathrm{u}+1.0086 \mathrm{u} \\ & =234.8203 \mathrm{u} \\ \mathrm{Q} & =(235.0439 \mathrm{u}-234.8203 \mathrm{u}) \mathrm{c}^2 \\ & =0.2236 \mathrm{c}^2 \\ & =0.2236 \times 931 \\ \mathrm{Q} & =208.1716 \end{aligned}$
